title: Polyhedra and Parameter Spaces for Matroids over Valuation Rings
overall_summary: Fink and Moci develop cryptomorphic axiom systems, polyhedral models, and moduli descriptions for matroids over a valuation ring. The core technical device is to encode a finitely presented module N by the sequence t_i(N), the minimum length after quotienting by i elements, and to show that the axioms for matroids over valuation rings are exactly a web of tropical Plücker-type inequalities obtained from one four-term Plücker relation by genericisation and zeroisation. The paper proves that every such matroid determines many incident tropical linear spaces, organized as flags, and that parameter spaces of matroids over a valuation ring are intersections of ordinary Dressians with explicit linear spaces and are closely related to tropical Bott-Samelson varieties. For the original problem, the most relevant material is the paper's treatment of tropical incidence, flag Dressians, containment loci in Dressians, stable sum/intersection constructions, and the way contraction, loops, generic extensions, and duality are encoded in tropical Plücker data.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2, with Lemma 2.3 as the module-theoretic input
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R be a valuation ring, let E be a finite ground set, and for every subset A⊆E and integer i≥0 let t_i(A) be an element of val(R)∪{∞}. These data are exactly the invariants t_i(A)=min length(M(A)/⟨x_1,...,x_i⟩), where the minimum is over x_1,...,x_i∈M(A), of a finitely presented matroid M over R if and only if the following hold. First, for every A, the sequence t_i(A) stabilizes at 0. Second, for all A and i, t_i(A)-t_{i+1}(A) ≥ t_{i+1}(A)-t_{i+2}(A). Third, for all A⊆E, b∈E\A, and i≥0, t_i(A)-t_{i+1}(A) ≥ t_i(A∪{b})-t_{i+1}(A∪{b}) ≥ t_{i+1}(A)-t_{i+2}(A). Fourth, for all A⊆E, b,c∈E\A, and i≥0, t_{i+1}(A)-t_{i+1}(A∪{b})-t_{i+1}(A∪{c})+t_i(A∪{b,c}) ≥ min{t_i(A∪{b})-t_{i+1}(A∪{b}), t_i(A∪{c})-t_{i+1}(A∪{c})}, with equality when the two terms inside the minimum are unequal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first passes from the t_i invariants to a more discrete family d_ell(N)=min{i: t_i(N)-t_{i+1}(N)<ell} and d_{≤ell}(N), the Grothendieck class of N/I_ell N, for ell∈val(R)^+. Lemma 2.3 characterizes matroids over R in terms of these d-invariants: d_ell(A) must be bounded above and nonincreasing in ell; adjoining one element changes d_ell by 0 or 1; and the two-element square must satisfy a submodularity inequality d_{≤ell}(A)-d_{≤ell}(A b)-d_{≤ell}(A c)+d_{≤ell}(A b c)≥0, with equality when d_ell(A b)≠d_ell(A c). Necessity of the two-element inequality comes from choosing x,y∈M(A) giving the quotients M(A b), M(A c), and M(A b c), reducing modulo I_ell, and using the exact 3-by-3 diagram whose corner term is Rx∩Ry; the Grothendieck class of this intersection is the defect in the inequality. Equality when d_ell(A b)≠d_ell(A c) follows because a nonzero intersection forces suitable small multiples of the cyclic submodules generated by x and y to coincide, forcing equal d_ell values. Sufficiency is proved by explicitly choosing generators e_ell and epsilon_ell for prescribed cyclic summands of M(A), and then constructing x and y as sums with coefficients tau(ell-d_{≤ell}(...)); these coefficients are chosen in R with prescribed valuations and multiplicative compatibility. The quotient modules by x, by y, and by x,y have the desired isomorphism types. Finally, the equivalence between the d-conditions and the displayed t-conditions is the identity t_i(A)-t_{i+1}(A)≥ell if and only if d_ell(A)>i; the hardest translation is the two-element condition, where the possible positions of ell relative to the adjacent t-differences are split into cases and reduced to the displayed inequality plus the one-element inequalities.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1 and Proposition 3.2, generic quotients and generic extensions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a finitely presented module N over a valuation ring R, let C be the largest cyclic direct summand of N. The elements n∈N that do not generate a cyclic direct summand isomorphic to C form the proper submodule N_gen=mN+{n∈N: rn=0 for some r not in Ann(N)}, where m is the maximal ideal of R. The quotient N_gq is defined as the complementary direct summand to C, and is called a quotient by a generic element. If M is a matroid over R on ground set E, then the assignment M_ge on E∪{g} given by M_ge(A)=M(A) when g∉A and M_ge(A)=M(A\{g})_gq when g∈A is again a matroid over R. The paper also notes the opposite operation of adding a loop, realized as direct sum with the one-element matroid L satisfying L(∅)=L({0})=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 3.1, the number of cyclic summands of N is dim_{R/m}(N/mN). If n∈mN, quotienting by n does not reduce this number, so n cannot generate a largest cyclic direct summand. If n has annihilator larger than Ann(N), it also cannot generate a copy of C. Conversely, if n is outside both obstructions, then ⟨n⟩ is isomorphic to C and the inclusion splits by lifting a splitting modulo m. For Proposition 3.2, one must verify the matroid axiom for any square involving two elements. Given x,y∈M(A) representing quotienting by b and c, it suffices to choose z∈M(A) whose image gives a generic quotient simultaneously in M(A), M(A)/⟨x⟩, M(A)/⟨y⟩, and M(A)/⟨x,y⟩. Since existence of matroids over a valuation ring depends only on the value group, the proof assumes an infinite residue field. Lemma 3.1 says that the bad choices of z form a proper vector subspace over the residue field in each of the four modules, hence their preimages in M(A) form finitely many proper subspaces; an element outside their union gives the required simultaneous generic quotient.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4 and the genericisation/zeroisation framework
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any matroid M over a valuation ring R, any subset A⊆E, any four elements b,c,d,e∈E\A, and any i≥0, the tropical Plücker relation min{t_i(A∪{b,c})+t_i(A∪{d,e}), t_i(A∪{b,d})+t_i(A∪{c,e}), t_i(A∪{b,e})+t_i(A∪{c,d})} is attained at least twice. Moreover, if a true statement in the t_i(A) variables is genericised along an element b by replacing t_i(S∪{b}) with t_{i+1}(S), or zeroised along b by replacing t_i(S∪{b}) with t_i(S), the resulting statement is again true for all matroids over R. Iterating these two operations on the four-term tropical Plücker relation produces the system of relations labeled D4, D3, D3', D2, D2', D2'', D1', D1'', and D0''; among these, D0'' is the concavity axiom T0, D1' and D1'' are the one-element inequalities T1, and D2' is the two-element axiom T2. Matroid duality transforms the invariants by t_i(M(A))=t_{|A|-r+i}(M^*(E\A)), where r is the generic rank of M, and exchanges the corresponding genericised and zeroised relations.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The four-term tropical Plücker relation follows by the proof of Proposition 5.6 of the earlier Fink-Moci paper on matroids over rings, using the t_i invariants and the axioms of Proposition 2.2. Genericisation is justified by Proposition 3.2: the generic extension M_ge has t_i(A∪{g})=t_{i+1}(A), so any universal identity applied to M_ge and then specializing b to g gives the genericised identity. Zeroisation is justified by adding a loop, for which t_i(A∪{0})=t_i(A). Since these two specializations commute, all iterated specializations of D4 hold. The paper organizes them in a diagram whose horizontal symmetries are induced by duality via the displayed formula for t_i under M↦M^*.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.3 and Corollaries 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, 4.8 on spannable and bispannable matroids
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A matroid M over R is called spannable if M(E)=0, cospannable if its dual is spannable, and bispannable if both hold. If M is spannable, then for every proper A⊊E there exists b∈E\A such that M(A∪{b}) is a generic quotient of M(A). Consequently, for all A⊆E and i≥0, t_{i+1}(M(A))=min_{b∉A} t_i(M(A∪{b})). In particular, a spannable matroid over R is determined by the values t_0(A)=length(M(A)) for all A⊆E. More generally, if M(E) can be presented with ell generators, then the same minimum formula holds for i≥ell and M is determined by the t_i(A) with 0≤i≤ell. If M is bispannable and M(∅)≅R^r, then M is determined by the modules M(A) with |A|=r.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key local fact is Lemma 4.2: for a two-element matroid, if neither one-element quotient is generic, then the two-element quotient is not a generic quotient of either one-element quotient. This follows from the explicit submodule N_gen of nongeneric elements in Lemma 3.1, because images of nongeneric elements remain nongeneric after quotienting by another nongeneric element. Lemma 4.3 then argues by maximal counterexample: if A is maximal such that no b∉A gives a generic quotient, then |A|≤|E|-2; choosing b∉A and applying Lemma 4.2 to every c∉A b shows A b is also a counterexample, contradiction. Since quotienting by a generic element simultaneously minimizes all t_i among cyclic quotients, the minimum formula follows. Determination by t_0 is obtained by iterating the formula. The ell-generator version applies the spannable result to the ell-fold generic extension of M. The bispannable statement combines the spannable reconstruction below rank r with the same reconstruction applied to the dual above rank r.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.3, polyhedral cryptomorphism for matroids over height-one valuation rings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume R is a height-one valuation ring, so val(R)⊆R∪{∞}. For a matroid M over R on ground set E, define P(M)=conv{(e_A,i,t_i(A)): A⊆E, i≥0, t_i(A)<∞}+cone{(0,0,1)} inside R^E×R^2, where e_A is the indicator vector of A. The recession cone of P(M) is cone{(0,1,0),(0,0,1)}. Let π:R^E×R^2→R^E×R be projection to the first two factors, and let D={(e_a,0): a∈E}∪{(0,0),(0,1)}. Every edge of P(M) projects under π to a direction e-e' with e,e'∈D. Conversely, if a polyhedron P⊆R^E×R^2 has recession cone cone{(0,1,0),(0,0,1)}, contains [0,1]^E×[i,∞)×[0,∞) for some i, has all vertices in {0,1}^E×N×val(R), has vertical minima over every (e_A,i) lying in val(R), and has every edge projecting to a direction e-e' with e,e'∈D, then P=P(M) for a matroid M over R.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The forward direction uses Lemma 5.4, a generalized symmetric basis exchange statement for the function t(v)=t_i(A) on lattice points v=(e_A,i). If v,w are two consecutive projected lattice points on an edge and v-w is not an allowed root direction, Lemma 5.4 supplies another pair v'=v-e_a+e_b and w'=w+e_a-e_b with v'+w'=v+w and t(v)+t(w)≥t(v')+t(w'). A linear functional minimized on the edge then also minimizes at the midpoint of the lifted points over v' and w'. Since the edge is not vertical in the last coordinate, this forces the lifted points over v' and w' to lie on the same edge, contradicting one-dimensionality or collinearity. For the converse, define t_i(A) as the minimum last coordinate of P over (e_A,i). The tail condition gives stabilization at zero, and convexity gives T0. To prove T1, restrict P to the three-dimensional face in which all coordinates except x_b are fixed; the regular subdivision of {e_A,e_{A b}}×N cannot have edges crossing the relevant triangles in forbidden directions, forcing triples of lifted points to lie on common faces and yielding the two one-element inequalities. To prove T2, restrict to the four-dimensional face involving b and c; the regular subdivision of the corresponding three-dimensional prism/octahedron is constrained by the same edge-direction rule. The center of the octahedron has three opposite-vertex decompositions, and if exactly one pair had minimal average height, the corresponding forbidden edge would appear. Hence the minimum is attained at least twice, which is T2. Proposition 2.2 then gives the matroid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.7, behavior of P(M) under deletion, contraction, duality, and direct sum
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid over a height-one valuation ring R on ground set E, and let x_a be the coordinate indexed by a∈E. If a∈E, then P(M\a)=π_a(P(M)∩{x_a=0}) and P(M/a)=π_a(P(M)∩{x_a=1}), where π_a forgets the x_a coordinate. If M has generic rank r, then P(M^*)=s(P(M)), where s:R^E×R^2→R^E×R^2 is the affine involution s(x_1,...,x_n,i,y)=(1-x_1,...,1-x_n, x_1+...+x_n-r+i, y). If M_1 and M_2 are matroids over R on ground sets E_1 and E_2, then P(M_1⊕M_2)=ι_1(P(M_1))+ι_2(P(M_2)), where the inclusions ι_j put the corresponding R^{E_j} coordinates into R^{E_1∪E_2} and are the identity on the last two coordinates.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Deletion and contraction simply select the vertices with x_a=0 or x_a=1, respectively, and then forget x_a. Duality follows from the identity t_i(M(A))=t_{|A|-r+i}(M^*(E\A)), which transforms the indicator vector e_A to 1-e_A and shifts i by |A|-r. For direct sums, the module identity is t_i(N⊕N')=min_{j+j'=i}(t_j(N)+t_{j'}(N')), because a generic quotient of a direct sum by i elements can be realized by quotienting cyclic summands from the two summands separately. This minimum is exactly the height formula for the Minkowski sum of the lifted polyhedra.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.8, base-change matroids as faces/projections of P(M)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid over a height-one valuation ring R with maximal ideal m. The spanning-set polytope of the residue matroid M⊗_R R/m is identified with P(M)∩(R^E×{(0,0)}), after forgetting the last two coordinates. The spanning-set polytope of the generic matroid M⊗_R Frac(R) is π(P(M)∩(R^E×{0}×R)), where π forgets the last coordinate.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For an ordinary matroid viewed over a field, spanning sets are exactly those subsets whose associated quotient module is zero. For the residue field, N⊗_R R/m=0 if and only if N=0, equivalently t_0(N)=0, so spanning sets are the vertices (e_A,0,0). For the fraction field, N⊗_R Frac(R)=0 if and only if N has no free cyclic summand, equivalently t_0(N)<∞; thus the relevant sets are precisely those for which some point (e_A,0,y) occurs in P(M), and projecting away y gives the spanning-set polytope.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.9 and Proposition 5.10, faces of P(M) and cyclic flats
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Faces F of P(M) are classified by their recession cone, a face of cone{(0,1,0),(0,0,1)}. If rec(F)=cone{(0,1,0),(0,0,1)}, then F is exactly the intersection of P(M) with equations x_a=0 and x_c=1; up to translation these are polytopes P(N) for minors N of M. If rec(F)=cone{(0,1,0)}, then F is such a minor face further intersected with the hyperplane t=0. If rec(F)=cone{(0,0,1)}, then the projections of these faces to the x-coordinates form the regular subdivision of the cube [0,1]^E induced by the corank function of the generic matroid M⊗_R Frac(R). If F is bounded and is a facet, then its projection to x-coordinates is a facet of the regular subdivision of the cube induced by S↦length(M(S)⊗_R R/I), for a nonzero proper finitely generated ideal I determined by the slope of the supporting hyperplane in the (i,t)-plane. In the special corank subdivision of [0,1]^E for an ordinary matroid, the facets are indexed by cyclic flats S and are conv{e_{S∪A\B}: A⊆E\S, B⊆S, cork(S∪A\B)=cork(S)-|A|}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For faces with full recession cone, projection to R^E is a face of the unit cube, hence is cut out by equations x_a=0 and x_c=1; Proposition 5.7 identifies these with deletion and contraction minors. The cone{(0,1,0)} case adds t=0. For cone{(0,0,1)}, the projection to R^E×R records the lower hull of the corank function of the generic matroid, hence gives its regular cube subdivision. Bounded facets have supporting hyperplanes whose restriction to the (i,t)-plane has slope corresponding to an ideal I, and the induced height function is length after tensoring with R/I. Proposition 5.10 analyzes the corank subdivision using the allowed edge directions: after embedding the cube by x↦(x,-sum x_a), each facet is a generalized permutahedron. A full-dimensional unimodular simplex in the facet forces the derivatives of the affine height function to be integers, and comparing adjacent vertices forces each derivative to be 0 or -1. Let S be the elements with derivative 0; the facet consists of the minima of cork(A)-|A∩(E\S)|. Being full-dimensional forces S to be both a flat and cyclic, and conversely a cyclic flat yields such a facet.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 6.1 and surrounding conventions, tropical Plücker vectors, tropical flag Plücker vectors, Dressians, and flag Dressians
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a ground set E=[n]. A tropical flag Plücker vector is a vector p=(p_A) indexed by all A⊆E with entries in ±val(R)∪{∞}, such that for all A,B⊆E with |A|≥|B|+2 the tropical incidence relation min_{a∈A\B}(p_{A\{a}}+p_{B∪{a}}) is attained at least twice, and for every 0≤r≤n some p_A with |A|=r is finite. A tropical Plücker vector of rank r is the same type of vector restricted to |A|=r, satisfying the relations with |A|-1=|B|+1=r and not all entries infinite. A tropical Plücker vector p defines a tropical linear space L(p), and L(p)=L(q) if and only if p and q differ by a global additive constant. The Dressian Dr(r,n) is the tropical projective parameter space of rank-r tropical linear spaces on [n]. The complete flag Dressian FD(n)=FD(1,...,n-1;n) is the parameter space of complete flags of incident tropical linear spaces.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is primarily definitional. The paper records that every tropical Plücker vector appears as the rank-r projection of some tropical flag Plücker vector, and it uses the convention that tropical addition is minimum and that a tropical polynomial vanishes when its minimum is attained at least twice.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.2, three-term Plücker relations suffice without infinite coordinates
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p=(p_A) be a vector indexed by r-subsets of [n], with no coordinate equal to ∞. If for every (r-2)-subset A and all b,c,d,e∉A the three-term tropical Plücker relation min{p_{A∪{b,c}}+p_{A∪{d,e}}, p_{A∪{b,d}}+p_{A∪{c,e}}, p_{A∪{b,e}}+p_{A∪{c,d}}} is attained at least twice, then p is a tropical Plücker vector.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The lemma invokes Speyer's definition of tropical linear spaces, where for finite-valued Plücker coordinates the three-term relations are used, together with Speyer's polyhedral characterization of valuated matroids. In the terminology of this paper, this equivalence is the valuated-matroid edge-direction characterization recalled as Theorem 5.1. Thus the three-term relations force the lifted hypersimplex to have the required matroidal edge directions, which is equivalent to all tropical Plücker relations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.3, three-term incidence relations suffice without infinite coordinates
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p and q be tropical Plücker vectors of respective ranks r and r+1 on a common ground set E, and assume neither has infinite coordinates. Suppose that for every (r-1)-subset A and b,c,d∈E\A the three-term tropical incidence relation min{q_{A∪{b,c}}+p_{A∪{d}}, q_{A∪{b,d}}+p_{A∪{c}}, q_{A∪{c,d}}+p_{A∪{b}}} is attained at least twice. Then the tropical linear spaces L(p) and L(q) are incident, i.e. they form adjacent ranks in a tropical flag.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Adjoin one new element * to E and define a rank-(r+1) vector f on E∪{*} by f_A=q_A if *∉A and f_A=p_{A\{*}} if *∈A. The three-term Plücker relations for f either are the ordinary three-term Plücker relations for p, the ordinary three-term Plücker relations for q, or exactly the displayed three-term incidence relations between p and q. Since all coordinates are finite, Lemma 6.2 implies f is a tropical Plücker vector. The full Plücker relations for f that involve * are precisely the general incidence relations between p and q, so p and q are incident.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.4, matroids over valuation rings produce flags of tropical linear spaces
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid over a valuation ring R on ground set E=[n]. For integers r,i with r+i at least the generic rank of M, define t_{r,i}=t_{r,i}(M) to be the vector indexed by r-subsets A⊆E with coordinate (t_{r,i})_A=t_i(M(A)). Then every t_{r,i} is a tropical Plücker vector of rank r. Moreover, whenever i_0,...,i_n are indices satisfying i_r-i_{r+1}∈{0,1} for each r, the tropical linear spaces form a full flag L(t_{0,i_0})⊆L(t_{1,i_1})⊆...⊆L(t_{n,i_n}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Plücker relation D4 from Proposition 3.4 gives the three-term tropical Plücker relations for each t_{r,i}. The incidence relations between consecutive ranks with the same i are D3, while those with i decreased by one are D3'. To handle possible infinite coordinates, the proof tensors M with R/I_k for a descending sequence of ideals I_k with intersection zero. The invariants t_i(M⊗R/I_k(A)) are finite and converge to t_i(M(A)), with coordinates tending to ∞ exactly when the limit is infinite. Lemmas 6.2 and 6.3 give tropical Plücker vectors and incidence for each finite k. Incidence between nonadjacent pairs in the desired flag is proved inductively by adding formal generic and zero elements and using all nine D-relations from Section 3; then closedness of the flag Dressian as a tropical prevariety passes the result to the limit.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.5, incidence with a neighboring tropical linear space forces Plücker relations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p be a tropical Plücker vector of rank r-1 on [n], and let q be a vector indexed by r-subsets of [n]. If p and q satisfy the tropical incidence relations for adjacent ranks, then q is a tropical Plücker vector. Dually, the same holds when p has rank r+1 and q has rank r, because the incidence relations are self-dual.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by contrapositive. If q is not a tropical Plücker vector, then by the polyhedral characterization of valuated matroids the lifted hypersimplex P(q)=conv{(e_A,q_A): |A|=r}+cone{(0,1)} has an edge between vertices over A and B whose direction e_A-e_B is not of the allowed form e_i-e_j. Build a combined polyhedron P(p,q) from the lifted points of p and q. Since P(q) is a face of P(p,q) maximizing the coordinate sum, the forbidden edge remains an edge of P(p,q). A two-dimensional face containing this edge and a vertex from the p-level is considered. If the p-level intersection of this face were an edge, it would be parallel to the forbidden edge, contradicting the edge-direction theorem for p. Hence the p-level intersection is a single vertex (e_C,p_C). At least one of A or B does not contain C; assume A does not. Then p_C+q_A appears in an incidence relation. The edge between (e_C,p_C) and (e_A,q_A) would make the corresponding midpoint uniquely minimal, contradicting the tropical incidence relation, which requires the minimum to be attained by another pair with the same midpoint.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 6.6, tropical convexity of adjacent containment loci
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a tropical linear space of rank r-1. The set of rank-r tropical linear spaces containing it, viewed as a subset of the Dressian, is tropically convex. Dually, fixing a rank-(r+1) tropical linear space, the set of rank-r tropical linear spaces contained in it is tropically convex.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: With the fixed rank-(r-1) Plücker vector p, the incidence relations are tropical linear equations in the coordinates q of the rank-r vector. Each such equation cuts out a tropical linear space, hence a tropically convex set, and intersections of tropically convex sets are tropically convex. Lemma 6.5 shows that after imposing these incidence relations, q automatically satisfies the tropical Plücker relations, so no extra non-convex Dressian equations need to be imposed.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7.1, parameter spaces as linear sections of Dressians
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R-Mat(r,n;k,ell) denote the set of essential matroids M over R on ground set [n] of generic rank r such that M(∅) can be presented with at most r+k generators and M([n]) can be presented with at most ell generators. Essential means that M([n]) has no nontrivial projective direct summand. Then R-Mat(r,n;k,ell) is the intersection of the Dressian Dr(n+k,2n+k+ell) with an explicit linear space. More precisely, partition [2n+k+ell] into E=[n], a set G of size r+k, and a set Z of size n-r+ell. The embedding sends M to the rank-(n+k) tropical Plücker vector p with p_A=t_{|A∩G|}(M(A∩E)) for |A|=n+k. The linear space identifies p_A and p_B whenever |G∩A|=|G∩B| and |Z∩A|=|Z∩B|, and imposes the terminal equalities encoding stabilization at zero, including the equality of all p_A with A∩Z=∅ and the analogous equality when all generic directions have been exhausted.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Plücker relations in Dr(n+k,2n+k+ell), when grouped according to how many entries lie in E, G, and Z, are exactly the relation D4 and all its genericisations and zeroisations from Section 3. Those relations, together with the stabilization condition TS, are an axiom system for matroids over R by Proposition 2.2 and the discussion in Section 3. The displayed formula for p_A encodes elements of G as generic elements and elements of Z as loops or zeroised elements. The linear equalities are precisely the assertion that p_A depends only on A∩E and the number of generic elements selected, not on the names of elements of G or Z, plus the conditions forcing sufficiently many quotients to become zero. Projectivization does not identify distinct matroids because the p_A with A∩Z=∅ are normalized to zero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7.5, fibers of the map to the Bott-Samelson flag data
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For fixed r,n,k,ell, let π send a matroid M∈R-Mat(r,n;k,ell) to the sequence of full flags of tropical linear spaces built from the vectors t_{s,i}(M) according to the Bott-Samelson word of equation (7.1). Let bπ(M)=(π(M), M(∅), M([n])). A pair (s,i) with 0<s<n and i≥0 is called exceptional for M if there exists μ∈val(R) such that t_i(M(A))=t_{i+1}(M(A))+μ for every s-subset A⊆[n]. Then every nonempty fiber of bπ is a bounded polytope, and its dimension is bounded above by both the exceptionality of the matroids in the fiber and by D(k,ell), where D(k,ell) is the number of integer pairs (s,i) with i<ell, s-r+i<k, i≥0, and 0<s<n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If M_1 and M_2 have the same π-image, then for each pair (s,i) the vectors t_{s,i}(M_1) and t_{s,i}(M_2) differ by a global additive constant λ_{s,i}; the endpoint data M(∅) and M([n]) force λ_{0,i}=λ_{n,i}=0. The fiber is cut out by linear equations fixing π and by the inequalities from Proposition 2.2; after fixing π, the nonlinear-looking T2 relation becomes linear because the relevant sums are already fixed by incidence. The constants λ_{s,i} are analyzed in right-to-left lexicographic order. If (s,i) is not exceptional, connectedness of the basis exchange graph gives adjacent s-subsets A b and A c for which t_i-t_{i+1} differs; applying D2' forces λ_{s,i} to be a function of already-determined later λ-values. If i≥ell, Corollary 4.6 gives λ_{s,i}=λ_{s-1,i+1}; the dual argument gives λ_{s,i}=λ_{s,i-1} when s-r+i≥k. Thus only the counted D(k,ell) exceptional positions can vary. Finally, T0 and the two-element relations bound each remaining λ above and below in terms of later constants, so the fiber is bounded.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.3, relation with tropical Bott-Samelson varieties
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume the residue field R/val^{-1}((0,∞]) is infinite. For the explicit word w in simple reflections from equation (7.1), formed from subwords x_{ij}=s_i s_{i+1}...s_j with first indices decreasing from r to 1 and then repeating 1 for k further steps and second indices repeating n-1 for ell+1 steps and then decreasing to n-r-k+ell, let Z_w be the classical Bott-Samelson variety for a fixed full flag and let ZD_w be the analogous Bott-Samelson Dressian of sequences of full flags of tropical linear spaces in which consecutive flags differ only in the rank prescribed by w. There exists a space Z'_w with Trop(Z_w)⊆Z'_w⊆ZD_w such that R-Mat(r,n;k,ell) projects piecewise linearly onto the intersection of Z'_w with a full-dimensional cone C. The fibers are polyhedral complexes of dimension at most D(k,ell)+k+ell, with D(k,ell) as in Proposition 7.5. In the bispannable case k=ell=0, the space R-Mat(r,n;0,0) is piecewise linearly isomorphic to Z'_w∩C.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The cone C is defined in the affine tropical coordinate space with coordinates p_{A,i} by the inequalities p_{A b,i+1}+p_{A,i}≥p_{A b,i}+p_{A,i+1}, p_{A b,i}+p_{A,i+2}≥p_{A b,i+1}+p_{A,i+1}, and p_{A,i}+p_{A,i+2}≥2p_{A,i+1}; these are the D1', D1'', and D0'' inequalities, so Lemma 7.4 gives R-Mat⊆C. The map π sends M to the sequence of flags whose rank-m component at stage j is L(t_{m,i(j,m)}(M)), where i(j,m) records how many times the simple reflection s_m has appeared in the prefix of w. Lemma 6.4 shows π(M)∈ZD_w. The proof defines Z'_w to be im(π)∪Trop(Z_w). Proposition 7.6 proves Trop(Z_w)∩C⊆im(π), so Z'_w∩C=im(π). The inclusion Trop(Z_w)⊆ZD_w follows from the fundamental theorem of tropical geometry because the incidence relations defining ZD_w tropicalize equations holding on Z_w. Proposition 7.5 bounds the fibers after remembering M(∅) and M([n]); forgetting those endpoint modules adds at most k+ell parameters. For k=ell=0 there are no additional endpoint parameters and no positive-dimensional exceptional fiber contribution.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7.6, tropicalized Bott-Samelson points in the cone come from matroids over R
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With the same parameters and infinite-residue-field hypothesis as Theorem 7.3, fix a full flag F over the fraction field of R whose top-aligned minors have valuation zero. Let Z_w be the classical Bott-Samelson variety attached to F. Then the part of Trop(Z_w) lying in the cone C of Theorem 7.3 is contained in the image of the map π from R-Mat(r,n;k,ell) to the Bott-Samelson Dressian.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Every point of Trop(Z_w) has a lift in the dense open subset of Z_w where consecutive flags actually differ at every prescribed step, using the fact that fibers of the tropicalization map are Zariski dense. Starting from the final fixed flag F, the proof constructs a matroid M over R recursively from right to left along the Bott-Samelson word. Suppose at one step the known flag has F^{m-1}⊆F^m⊆F^{m+1} and the previous flag replaces F^m by another m-plane G with F^{m-1}⊆G⊆F^{m+1}. Choose vectors v and v' such that F^m=F^{m-1}+Kv and G=F^{m-1}+Kv'. Consider the (m+1)-plane L=F^{m-1}×{(0,0)} + K(v,c,0)+K(v',0,c') in K^n×K^2, with constants c,c' chosen so that the valuations of the Plücker coordinates on the three already-known slices match the already assigned t-values. The remaining slice gives the new t_{m,i} values. Since L is an honest linear space, its Plücker valuations satisfy the tropical relation D2', hence the T2 axiom. The assumption that the tropical point lies in C gives T0 and T1, and the initial final flag gives stabilization TS. Proposition 2.2 then yields a matroid over R realizing the desired flag sequence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.10, commutation of stable sum and stable intersection in a one-element extension
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p be any tropical Plücker vector on [n]. Let g be a rank-1 tropical Plücker vector and h a rank-(n-1) tropical Plücker vector on [n] such that L(g)⊆L(h). Let v∈±val(R)∪{∞}. Extend g to a rank-1 vector g' on [n]∪{bar*} by setting g'_{bar*}=v and keeping g on [n]. Extend h to a rank-n vector h' on [n]∪{*} by setting h'_{[n]}=v and keeping h on subsets containing *. Then, with stable sum denoted + and stable intersection denoted ∩, one has (p+g)∩h'=(p∩h)+g'.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The stable sum and stable intersection are expanded from their definitions: (p+p')_A=min{p_B+p'_C: B disjoint union C=A}, and the dual formula for intersection. For all coordinates except those indexed by sets containing bar* but not *, equality of the two sides is immediate from the definitions. For a set A containing bar* but not *, the left side expands to min{((p+g)∩h)_{A\{bar*}}, p_{A\{bar*}}+v}, while the right side expands to min{((p∩h)+g)_{A\{bar*}}, p_{A\{bar*}}+v}. The first terms are equal because L(g)⊆L(h), so the two minima agree.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7.8, every tropical linear space occurs as t_{r,0} of a bispannable matroid over R
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p be a tropical Plücker vector of rank r on [n], normalized so that the minimum of its finite entries is 0. Then there exists a matroid M over R of generic rank r on [n] with t_{r,0}(M)=p. In fact M may be chosen in R-Mat(r,n;0,0), i.e. M is bispannable with M(∅) free and M([n])=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let 0 denote the all-zero rank-1 tropical Plücker vector and 0^* the all-zero rank-(|E|-1) tropical Plücker vector on the relevant ground set. Form a rank-n tropical Plücker vector q on 2n elements by q=p∩0^*∩...∩0^* + 0+...+0, using r stable intersections with 0^* and n-r stable sums with 0. Lemma 7.10 ensures that the order of these operations is compatible. Expanding the definitions gives q_A=min{p_B: |B|=r and |B∩A|≥r-|G∩A|}, where the 2n-element ground set is split into the original elements, generic elements G, and zero elements Z as in Proposition 7.1 with k=ell=0. Hence q depends only on the cardinalities relevant to the linear space W of Proposition 7.1; moreover when the terminal conditions apply, the minimum is the normalized value 0. Thus q lies in the linear section W of the Dressian, so by Proposition 7.1 it is the image of a bispannable matroid M over R. The coordinate corresponding to no added generic or zero modification recovers p as t_{r,0}(M).

proof_techniques.name: Encoding module quotients by the t_i sequence
proof_techniques.description: The paper replaces the isomorphism type of a finitely presented module N over a valuation ring by the sequence t_i(N)=min length(N/⟨x_1,...,x_i⟩). Differences t_i-t_{i+1} recover the cyclic summands of N, and the matroid-over-R axiom becomes a finite family of tropical inequalities in these t_i(A). This makes module-theoretic quotient data look like Plücker coordinates in adjacent ranks.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests encoding quotient-type tropical subspaces inside a fixed tropical linear space by auxiliary Plücker coordinates for generic or loop extensions. It may help translate poset operations on tropical linear subspaces into algebraic inequalities where an involution can be checked coordinatewise.

proof_techniques.name: Genericisation and zeroisation of Plücker relations
proof_techniques.description: A single tropical Plücker relation D4 produces a whole axiom system by replacing a ground-set element either with a generic element, which shifts t_i to t_{i+1}, or with a loop, which leaves t_i unchanged. These operations commute and are justified by actual matroid-over-ring extensions. The resulting relations include ordinary Plücker relations, incidence relations, submodularity inequalities, and their duals.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The natural inclusion of flats by F↦Trop(M/F⊕U_{0,F}) involves contraction and loops, exactly the kinds of operations controlled by zeroisation. This framework could be used to embed a relative Dressian or a flat-lattice interval into a larger ordinary Dressian where order reversal might become a duality or coordinate-complement operation.

proof_techniques.name: Polyhedral root-edge characterizations
proof_techniques.description: The lifted polyhedron P(M) of a matroid over a valuation ring is characterized by the fact that its projected edge directions are roots e-e' of a type-A system enlarged by two extra directions corresponding to genericisation and zeroisation. Conversely, these edge directions plus basic integrality and recession conditions reconstruct the matroid. Deletion, contraction, duality, and direct sum become face, affine reflection, and Minkowski-sum operations on P(M).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If tropical subspaces in Trop(M) are represented by valuated matroid polytopes or subdivisions, an order-reversing involution might be sought as an affine or polar transformation on such polyhedra. Proposition 5.7 is especially relevant because it gives the exact affine formula for duality at the P(M) level.

proof_techniques.name: Reducing incidence to three-term tropical relations
proof_techniques.description: For finite-valued Plücker coordinates, Lemmas 6.2 and 6.3 show that three-term Plücker and three-term incidence relations imply all Plücker and incidence relations. For coordinates with ∞, the paper uses base change modulo a descending sequence of ideals to approximate by finite-valued data and then takes limits in the closed flag Dressian.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Containment of tropical linear spaces is often encoded by incidence relations. These lemmas allow one to prove containment or flag properties using only local three-term equations, which may simplify verifying that a proposed involution sends contained subspaces to containing subspaces.

proof_techniques.name: Incidence with one neighbor forces the Dressian equations
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 6.5 proves that if a vector q satisfies incidence with a fixed neighboring tropical linear space p, then q automatically satisfies all tropical Plücker relations. The proof uses lifted hypersimplex edge directions: a failure of the Plücker property would create a forbidden edge, and a two-dimensional face would contradict one of the incidence relations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is directly useful for relative Dressian questions because it says certain containment loci can be cut out purely by incidence with a fixed space; no separate Dressian equations are needed. This can make the poset of subspaces inside a fixed tropical linear space more tractable.

proof_techniques.name: Tropical convexity of adjacent containment loci
proof_techniques.description: Corollary 6.6 shows that the set of rank-r tropical linear spaces containing a fixed rank-(r-1) space, or contained in a fixed rank-(r+1) space, is tropically convex. The reason is that the incidence equations are tropical linear in the moving Plücker vector and Lemma 6.5 automatically enforces the Dressian conditions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A relative Dressian Dr(M) is a containment space. Tropical convexity in adjacent-rank cases may provide a base case or local chart for constructing, extending, or obstructing an order-reversing involution.

proof_techniques.name: Bott-Samelson and flag-Dressian parameterization
proof_techniques.description: The paper encodes a matroid over a valuation ring as a sequence of complete flags of tropical linear spaces, where successive flags differ in only one rank. This is modeled by a tropical Bott-Samelson space ZD_w, and the actual matroid parameter space maps piecewise linearly onto a full-dimensional cone inside an intermediate space Z'_w between the tropicalization of a classical Bott-Samelson variety and ZD_w.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Chains of tropical subspaces inside a fixed Trop(M) can potentially be studied through flag Dressians and Bott-Samelson words. An order-reversing involution might correspond to reversing such a word, applying tropical duality to each flag, or combining both.

proof_techniques.name: Stable sum and stable intersection as constructive operations
proof_techniques.description: Stable sum and stable intersection of tropical Plücker vectors are used to construct a rank-n vector q on an enlarged ground set from an arbitrary rank-r tropical Plücker vector p. Lemma 7.10 gives a commutation rule under a containment hypothesis L(g)⊆L(h), and Proposition 7.8 uses this to show every tropical linear space occurs as t_{r,0} of a bispannable matroid over R.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Stable intersection is the tropical analogue of taking intersections or orthogonal complements under duality. These operations may provide explicit formulas for candidate complement maps on subspaces, or for constructing test cases in the relative Dressian.

proof_techniques.name: Controlling nonuniqueness by exceptional pairs
proof_techniques.description: The map from matroids over R to Bott-Samelson flag data is not always injective; the ambiguity is measured by exceptional pairs (s,i) for which t_{s,i} and t_{s,i+1} differ only by a global scalar. Proposition 7.5 shows that after fixing endpoints, fibers are bounded polytopes and gives a precise dimension bound by counting possible exceptional positions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proposed order-reversing operation is defined through flag or Plücker data, scalar ambiguities and exceptional loci may have to be controlled. This fiber analysis indicates where nonuniqueness can occur and how it is bounded.

other_useful_info: Example 7.7 shows that the Bott-Samelson Dressian incidence conditions D4, D3, and D3' together with the cone inequalities do not imply the D2' relation needed for matroids over a valuation ring; hence the image of the matroid parameter space in ZD_w is generally a proper subset. Question 7.9 asks whether every flag of tropical linear spaces in tropical projective space occurs as the flag L(t_{s,max(r-s,0)}(M)) for some matroid M over R. Questions 4.9, 4.10, and 4.11 ask for further cryptomorphisms for spannable matroids, including whether bispannable matroids over R are matroids over a suitable hyperring and how to characterize a rank-function-style polyhedral cone RP(M). The paper relies on Speyer's tropical linear spaces and valuated matroid polyhedra, Haque's flag Dressian, and standard Bott-Samelson varieties; these are the main cited external frameworks relevant to relative Dressian and flag-involution questions.
why_relevant: Gives polyhedral and moduli descriptions of valuated matroids and flags over valuation rings. Its tropical Bott-Samelson/flag viewpoint is relevant to quotient chains inside a fixed tropical linear space.
